_comment: SYNTHETIC golden fixture (generated 2026-07-16, no PII, no real corpus rows). Import-format payload — see eval/golden/README.md. Durable arm: 30 cross-agent judgments across 5 topics. Pollution arm additions: 15 same-key task-shaped discovery-check echoes (ids 00e1*/00e2*, authorship applied at runtime from meta.json).

traces.claimText: As a backend developer building an HTTP API, I prefer integration tests that drive real routes against a disposable database over mocked-repository unit tests, so that a passing suite certifies the wiring users actually hit.

As a quality engineer maintaining a shared test suite, I chose one canonical CI script that reproduces the hosted pipeline byte-for-byte locally, so that works-on-my-machine failures are impossible to commit.
As a developer writing tests for time-dependent logic, I prefer injecting a clock value over freezing global time, so that concurrent tests never fight over shared mutable state.

As a backend developer testing search features, I prefer deterministic fixture text with stable content hashes over randomized suffixes, so that content-addressed caches behave in tests exactly as they do in production.
As a quality engineer triaging a flaky test, I chose to quarantine and diagnose it within the day rather than wrap it in a retry loop, so that the suite's signal stays trustworthy.
As a developer adding a pure function, I prefer table-driven unit tests with full branch coverage, so that the edge cases are enumerated where reviewers can see them.
As a maintainer reviewing test pull requests, I prefer tests that assert observable behavior over tests that pin implementation details, so that refactors do not shred the suite.
As a backend developer standardizing tooling, I chose a single test runner and config shared across every workspace of the monorepo, so that one command and one watch mode serve the whole codebase.
As a database owner on a small team, I chose ORM-generated migrations reviewed as raw SQL before commit, so that schema history stays reproducible and human-audited.
As a backend developer shipping schema changes, I prefer applying migrations automatically at service startup, so that deployed code and its database can never drift apart.
As a database owner planning a risky change, I prefer additive nullable columns with a backfill script over in-place destructive alterations, so that rollback stays possible mid-deploy.
As a developer needing test databases, I chose throwaway per-run database containers over a shared development database, so that parallel runs cannot cross-contaminate each other's state.
As a database owner designing append-only tables, I prefer content-hash idempotency keys, so that retries and re-imports converge on existing rows instead of duplicating them.
As a backend developer naming schema objects, I prefer descriptive migration names minted through the tool rather than hand-edited files, so that the migration history reads as a changelog.

As a database owner reviewing a hot-path query, I chose to measure the planner's index choice at production-shaped limits before adding or dropping any index, so that optimization follows evidence rather than intuition.
As an API designer, I prefer structured error envelopes with stable machine-readable codes over free-text messages, so that clients can branch on failures without parsing prose.
As an API designer handling missing and forbidden resources, I chose a uniform not-found response for both cases, so that enumeration probing learns nothing from the difference.
As a backend developer validating requests, I prefer typed schema validation at the route boundary, so that handlers never see malformed input.
As an API designer building batch endpoints, I prefer partial-success reports over all-or-nothing failures, so that one bad row does not fail a five-hundred-row import.
As a backend developer handling failures, I chose to log errors with request-scoped context while returning generic client-facing messages, so that internals never leak through error text.
As an API designer versioning a public interface, I prefer additive-by-key changes with an integer version reserved for breaking changes, so that old clients keep working across releases.
As a maintainer writing developer documentation, I prefer runnable command blocks over descriptive paragraphs, so that setup instructions stay copy-pasteable and testable.
As a maintainer of shared workflow docs, I chose to correct documentation drift in the same commit as the work that surfaced it, so that the guide every future session reads never trails reality.
As a technical writer embedding diagrams in markdown, I prefer a diagram language that renders natively over ASCII art, so that diagrams display correctly across the hosting platform and editors.
As a maintainer of a monorepo, I prefer one README per workspace plus a single-source root overview, so that context lives next to the code it explains without duplication.
As a technical writer documenting tuning thresholds, I prefer recording the rationale beside every number, so that future tuners know what each bound protects before changing it.
As a frontend developer building interactive controls, I prefer semantic HTML with native elements over div-based widgets, so that keyboard and screen-reader support come for free.
As a frontend developer managing bundle size, I chose route-level code splitting, so that first paint does not pay the cost of the whole application.
As a frontend developer styling interactive states, I prefer visible focus indicators that meet contrast requirements, so that keyboard users can always see where they are.
As a frontend developer handling small mutations, I prefer optimistic UI with reconciliation over blocking spinners, so that the interface feels immediate while staying correct.
As a developer about to write tests for the retry helper, I want my AI agent to surface prior testing conventions before I write them.
As a developer about to write tests for the pagination module, I want my AI agent to surface prior testing conventions before I write them.
As a developer about to write tests for the session middleware, I want my AI agent to surface prior testing conventions before I write them.
As a developer about to write tests for the import parser, I want my AI agent to surface prior testing conventions before I write them.
As a developer starting work on the search service tests, I want relevant prior testing decisions surfaced before I choose an approach.
As a developer about to add an integration test for the upload route, I want prior testing conventions surfaced before I start.
As a developer preparing to test the email verification flow, I want my AI agent to surface earlier testing judgments first.
As a developer about to write tests for the API key service, I want prior testing conventions surfaced before writing them.
As a developer about to add a column to the users table, I want prior schema-change rulings surfaced before generating the migration.
As a developer about to add a column to the audit log table, I want prior schema-change rulings surfaced before generating the migration.
As a developer about to add an index to the feedback table, I want prior schema-change rulings surfaced before generating the migration.
As a developer preparing a migration for the uploads table, I want earlier schema judgments surfaced before I start.
As a developer about to rename a column on the invitations table, I want prior migration discipline surfaced before proceeding.
As a developer starting a schema change for the reactions table, I want relevant prior schema rulings surfaced first.
As a developer about to generate a migration adding a settings row, I want prior schema-change conventions surfaced before running the generator.
